title: Willow obelisk plant support
subtitle: A cone-shaped garden obelisk woven from fresh-cut willow rods for climbing plants
excerpt: A willow obelisk is a conical plant support built from a framework of upright stakes woven with horizontal willow rods, growing narrower toward a pointed top. It is used for climbing beans, sweet peas, and roses. Fresh-cut or recently harvested willow is essential: the rods must bend at sharp angles without cracking. A first large-scale willow weaving project.
sourceNotes: Willow garden obelisk and plant support forms documented in Victorian and Edwardian kitchen garden design; traditional in British kitchen gardens through the 20th century.

content.text: A willow obelisk is built and put in place in one session. The
stakes
are pushed into the garden bed, gathered at the top, and tied. Bands of
randing
go up the cone at set heights.
Fresh
willow is a must. The rods bend round the uprights without cracking, and they may root into the soil. The result is a living obelisk by the end of the growing season.
What you need: materials

For an obelisk approximately 120 cm tall and 40 cm in diameter at the base: 8 upright rods, 170 cm long and 8 to 10 mm in diameter, from straight hazel or long willow; and 20 to 30 thinner willow rods, 120 to 150 cm long, for the horizontal weaving. All rods should be freshly cut or soaked for at least two hours before use.
What you need: tools
Secateurs for cutting. A bodkin for tucking ends. Garden wire or willow withies for binding the top. That is all.
The work
Step 1: Push in the uprights

Mark a circle 40 cm in diameter on the ground where the obelisk is to stand. Push 8 upright rods into the soil at equal intervals around the circle to a depth of 25 to 30 cm, leaving 140 to 145 cm above the ground. Push them in at a very slight inward angle (about 5 degrees from vertical) so that they naturally converge toward the top.
Step 2: Gather and bind the top

Gather the tops of all 8 uprights together and bind tightly with garden wire or a thin willow withy wrapped multiple times and tucked under itself to secure. The bundle should be compact and tight; this is the structural apex of the cone.
Step 3: Weave horizontal bands
Start 20 cm from the ground. Weave a band of
randing

around all 8 uprights. Take a thin weaving rod and push one end behind a stake. Weave it in and out around the cone, over one upright and under the next. On 8 stakes (an even number), the pattern stays still rather than moving on. Use a simple over-one-under-one method all the way round. Snip the rod tip and tuck it behind a stake when you reach the end. Start the next rod at the next stake.

Work bands of three to four rounds at 20 cm intervals up the cone: one band at 20 cm, one at 45 cm, one at 70 cm, one at 90 cm. Each band gets narrower as the cone tapers; use shorter rods for the upper bands. Tuck the ends of each rod behind a stake with the bodkin to secure.
Step 4: Secure the base
Work a
waling

band at the lowest horizontal band level (20 cm from the ground) using three rods simultaneously for a stronger base. This locks the lowest band and prevents the uprights spreading further as the obelisk settles.
Finishing
The obelisk is ready for use immediately. If built from fresh willow and the uprights were pushed deep enough, some stakes will root and the obelisk will become a living structure by midsummer.
Variations
Make a pair at 80 cm height for a lower rose or clematis support frame.
Use hazel uprights instead of willow for a stiffer frame. It will not root, but it will last three to four seasons before the wood rots at the soil line.
Make a living willow arch by planting two short obelisks 80 cm apart. Weave their tops together. The willow will root and grow into a lasting arch.

items.fix: Re-push the base of any splaying upright further inward and retighten the top binding. The lowest waling band must be in place to resist further splaying.
items.cause: The uprights were pushed in vertically rather than at an inward angle, or the top binding is not tight enough to hold the cone shape.
items.symptom: The uprights splay outward rather than converging at the top

items.fix: Soak rods for a longer period before use. Use thinner, more flexible rods for the horizontal bands.
items.cause: The rods are too dry or too thick to bend sharply around the uprights.
items.symptom: Horizontal rods snap at the upright positions

items.fix: Push the leaning-side stakes deeper and pack the soil firmly around them.
items.cause: Stakes were pushed to different depths or the soil is soft on one side.
items.symptom: The obelisk leans to one side after a week
